extracted.brandBackground: Once Upon A Child is a retail franchise specializing in buying and selling quality used and new children's apparel, toys, equipment, furniture, and accessories.

leadership.name: Brett D. Heffes
leadership.role: Chair of the Board, Director and Chief Executive Officer
leadership.background: Elected Chair in March 2020 and has served as CEO since February 2016.
leadership.whyItMatters: Provides top-level executive leadership and strategic direction.

leadership.name: Renae M. Gaudette
leadership.background: Served as COO since February 2022 and has been with Winmark since 1995.
leadership.whyItMatters: Oversees day-to-day franchise operations and brand management.

leadership.name: Anthony D. Ishaug
leadership.background: Served as CFO since September 2008 and Treasurer since November 2009.
leadership.whyItMatters: Manages the financial strategy and fiscal health of the franchisor.

leadership.name: Lisa Hake
leadership.background: Served as CMO since October 2025, previously VP of Marketing at Great Clips.
leadership.whyItMatters: Directs brand marketing and promotional strategies.

leadership.name: Ryan D. Baune
leadership.background: Served as VP of Technology since February 2025, previously with Taco John's.
leadership.whyItMatters: Leads technology infrastructure and POS system development.

leadership.name: Bradley W. Spencer
leadership.background: Served as VP of Operations since March 2020, previously Director of Once Upon A Child Division.
leadership.whyItMatters: Directs operational support and field consulting for franchisees.

item19.notes: Gross profit figures reflect cost of sales but do not reflect operating expenses.

lineItems.notes: Non-refundable fee for a single store.
Includes shelving, counters, and other store fixtures.
Physical labor expense of building the store.
New and used children's apparel and goods.
Training travel, legal, internet, and pre-opening labor.
Estimated rent expense for the first three months.

hiddenCosts.name: Technology Fee
hiddenCosts.description: Periodic fee for technology-related services, estimated between $500 and $2,500 per year.

hiddenCosts.name: Computer Hardware Handling Fee
hiddenCosts.description: Approximately 4% handling fee on computer hardware component purchases.

operationalRisks.title: Out-of-State Dispute Resolution
operationalRisks.description: Disputes must be resolved by arbitration only in Minnesota, which may increase costs.

operationalRisks.title: Spousal Liability
operationalRisks.description: Spouses must sign a personal guarantee, placing personal and marital assets at risk.

operationalRisks.title: Minimum Advertising Payments
operationalRisks.description: Franchisees must make minimum advertising payments regardless of sales levels.

extracted.conceptRationale: Once Upon A Child is a retail store concept that buys and sells used and new children's products.
extracted.staffingRationale: The retail store model requires on-site staff to manage inventory, buy used goods, and assist customers.

riskReasons: A high-severity operational tripwire is disclosed — see the tripwires section.
Franchisor financial distress disclosed (negative net worth) — confirm its ability to support the system against the audited statements.

underwriting.assessment: With $250,000 liquid against a $420,800 mid-point build-out, there is a $170,800 capital gap. An SBA loan or an equity partner is required to do this deal. At mid-cohort revenue, modeled cash flow after debt service is about $94,554/mo before payroll and maintenance — a thin margin of error.

insights.conceptRationale: Once Upon A Child is a retail store concept that buys and sells used and new children's products.

benchmark.label: Retail / product
benchmark.dominantRisk: Gross margin on product (high COGS) and inventory turns. Thin margins amplify any sales miss.
benchmark.rampNote: 3–12 months to run-rate depending on category.
considerations: Budget COGS at 45–65% of sales — product margin is the whole game here.
Verify inventory turns and markdown/shrink rates with Item 20 operators.
Confirm minimum inventory and required reorder commitments in Item 8/Item 11.

crossCheck.message: No operating-EBITDA margin is disclosed in Item 19. The 5–12% band below is an industry estimate for this concept — validate it against real franchisee P&Ls (Item 20) before relying on any margin.

buildup.label: − Labor
buildup.note: conventionally staffed · ≈ 3.1–5.5 FTE at ~$20/hr fully loaded

assumptions.detail: Classified as “Retail / product” from the FDD (Item 1 / brand description).
Taken from the FDD (~$8,333/mo); already netted in margin after fees & rent.
Estimated at the 45–65% category band — COGS is never disclosed in an FDD.
Estimated at the 10–18% conventionally staffed band — labor is never disclosed in an FDD.
Utilities, insurance, and R&M estimated at 6–10% (category catch-all).
Our calculation — the disclosed top line minus the benchmarked costs above. Not a figure stated in the FDD.

insights.consultCtaPitch: These are category benchmarks applied to the disclosed top line — your unit's real costs will differ, and that difference is the conversation. Want them pressure-tested against actual franchisee P&Ls for your territory?
insights.asOf: 2026 — general industry benchmarks, refine against real franchisee P&Ls
insights.disclaimer: Industry benchmark ranges for your own budgeting and Item 20 questions — NOT a projection of this franchise's results, and not investment advice.

financialCondition.headline: This franchisor has negative net worth (about $53.68M in the red) — it owes more than it owns — though the trend isn't worsening. Understand what's funding the gap before you commit.
financialCondition.context: Worth perspective: the auditor issued a clean (unmodified) opinion and did not raise going-concern doubt; revenue is growing; the business is profitable. Early-stage franchisors commonly run losses and carry a deficit while investing to scale — this reads more like spending ahead of revenue than a failing business. The real question is runway: how the gap is funded, and for how long.
body: What we found (2025-12-27, audited by Grant Thornton LLP): Net income of $41.65M on $86.06M of revenue — roughly 0.5x spent per $1 earned. Negative net worth (a members'/stockholders' deficit) of $53.68M. Total liabilities are about 3.2x total assets. Cash of $10.30M against $5.67M of obligations due within a year (a current ratio of 2.49). Note: about 13% of those liabilities is deferred revenue — prepaid franchise fees the company owes as future services, not borrowed money. Netting it out, liabilities are still about 2.8x assets.
Why it matters to you: field support, training, technology, and marketing come out of the franchisor's budget. If outside funding slows, those are the first things to get cut.

Verify before you commit: ask for the most recent interim (unaudited) financials and the current-year funding plan; ask whether the parent will sign a guarantee of performance; and cross-check Item 20 — unit closures, transfers, and terminations are the operational tell that corroborates financial stress.
aggravators: negative net worth — it owes more than it owns
mitigants: the auditor issued a clean (unmodified) opinion and did not raise going-concern doubt
financialCondition.evidenceNote: Flagged on the FDD's "Special Risks" page and grounded in the Item 21 / Exhibit F audited financials. Figures are extracted; all ratios and grading are computed. Confirm against the source statements and a CPA before relying on them.
